hypothesis: A step-by-step wizard with explicit progress, back/edit controls, per-step validation, and safe save/resume reduces cognitive load without hiding status or trapping keyboard and screen-reader users.
interaction_paradigm: A true step-by-step wizard: exactly one primary task step (Contact, Delivery address, Payment, Review & place order) is the active step at a time, with explicit named progress ("Step N of 4: <name>"), a single active keyboard-safe Continue control per frontier step, Back and Edit controls to return to any completed step, and resumable/safe local save state. Steps not yet reached remain in the DOM but are hidden accessibly (aria-hidden, removed from tab order, visually collapsed) until the wizard journey reaches them; once a step is reached it stays reachable/editable rather than being torn out of the page, so earlier answers remain visible and correctable without ever navigating backward through a forward-only revealer.

human_use.rationale: The Artifact is a human-operated mobile checkout. Its success depends on discoverability, navigation, input burden, error recovery, feedback, accessibility, touch ergonomics, interruption recovery, perceived latency, destructive-action safety, and cognitive load across cross-disability use.

qualitative_lenses_note: These lenses are qualitative only in this metadata and in self-observation.md; no objective ergonomics claims are derived from them. Only the frozen harness's pass/fail assertions and metrics below are treated as objective evidence.

commands.note: First candidate: strict single-step-visible design. 5 of 7 gates failed (content-fidelity, semantic-accessibility-gate, error-recovery-gate, resilience-gate, offline-safety-gate). Evidence preserved untouched in evidence-attempt-1/.

Second candidate: rearchitected to progressive-disclosure wizard (revealed steps stay reachable; single frontier Continue control); fixed shipping-hook text, primary heading, guest-checkout text, and non-text-control-contrast. 2 of 7 gates still failed (semantic-accessibility-gate: focus-contrast too low on dark-green buttons; offline-safety-gate: invalid-or-unreviewed-placement-blocked reported 1 instead of 0). Evidence preserved untouched in evidence-attempt-2/.

Third candidate: fixed .btn-primary:focus outline-color (was too low contrast against the dark-green button background) and made the confirmation number populate only via JS on real placement (the static HTML previously always contained the literal confirmation text, which the harness's inner_text()-based confirmation_count() read even while the confirmation region was hidden). All 7 gates passed; blocking_failure=false. Preserved as an additional verification run in evidence-attempt-3/.
Final, required run using the exact --out path specified by the task. All 7 gates passed; blocking_failure=false. This is the authoritative evidence directory for this Loop.

lesson: The frozen harness's reveal() helper is strictly forward-only (it only ever presses the 'continue' hook, never 'back'). A wizard design where each step is torn away/re-hidden once the user advances past it is therefore structurally incompatible with gates that need to re-fill or re-check an earlier step's fields after the wizard has moved on (error-recovery-gate's preserve-valid-safe-value, resilience-gate's reduced-motion-completes-once). The fix is a 'progressive disclosure' wizard: once a step is reached it stays visible and reachable forever (supporting edit-in-place and forward-only re-fill), while exactly one 'frontier' step's Continue control is ever visible, so reveal() always finds a single unambiguous next step. Two further pitfalls were non-obvious from the harness source alone and only surfaced empirically: (1) Playwright's inner_text() still returns an element's static text even when an ancestor carries the `hidden` attribute, so confirmation_count() will count a statically-present 'SYN-2048' string even before an order is really placed — the confirmation ID must be set by JS only at the moment of real placement, never present in the initial HTML. (2) a solid CSS :focus outline color that has strong contrast against a light page background can still fail the focus-contrast check on a differently-colored (e.g. dark) button background — focus-ring color needs to be verified per background, not assumed globally sufficient.
decision_note: All frozen objective gates pass with blocking_failure=false. No independent judge was run and no promotion/new_best/self-scoring claim is made, per task scope. This Loop's evidence is offered for independent human/judge review only.
